reason: The paper fits ranger random-forest models and reports a single, unqualified "variable importance" measure with no mention of permutation anywhere in the text; the only methodological caveat cited is Strobl and Zeileis (2008) on the known bias of impurity-type importance toward variables with more categories/continuous scale, which the authors address only by recoding continuous variables to categorical (a partial workaround, not a switch to permutation/conditional importance). The text also explicitly narrates the classic MDI interaction-credit artifact: "sector of occupation... is less important in the analysis covering only characteristics of the social situation, [but] it gains importance in the full model because of its interaction with the variables measuring the opportunity structure." The magnitude/ranking of this importance is the direct basis for the paper's central claims ("the social situation variables outperform... social groups," "opportunity structure... have lower predictive power") and the resulting urban/rural policy recommendations. The only validation offered is overall classification-error confusion matrices (Table 2), which check aggregate model accuracy, not the correctness of the specific feature ranking or selection; no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the ranked feature set is reported anywhere in the manuscript.
